The area of the rectangle at each stage is the sum of the areas of the squares added up to that stage. At Stage 1, there is 1 square with area $3^2 = 9$ square inches. At Stage 2, there are 2 squares, one with area $3^2 = 9$ square inches and the other with area $2 \cdot 3^2 = 18$ square inches. In general, at Stage $n$, there are $n$ squares, each with area $3^2 = 9$ square inches, so the total area is $9n$ square inches. Therefore, at Stage 6, the area is $9 \cdot 6 = 54$ square inches. </think> <answer> 54 </answer>

Therefore, the area of the rectangle at Stage 6 is 54 square inches. </think> <answer> 54 </answer>

The area at stage \( n \) is calculated by adding a \( 3 \times 3 \) square at each stage. At stage 1, the area is \( 3 \times 3 = 9 \) square inches. At stage 3, another \( 9 \) square inches is added, making the total \( 18 + 9 = 27 \) square inches. Following this pattern, the area at stage \( n \) can be expressed as \( 9n \) square inches. Therefore, at stage 6, the area is \( 9 \times 6 = 54 \) square inches. </think> <answer> 54 </answer>

Following this pattern, at Stage 6, the total area will be the sum of the areas of the squares added at each stage, which is \(9 + 9 + 9 + 9 + 9 + 9 = 54 \text{ square inches}\). \[ \text{Therefore, the area of the rectangle at Stage 6 is } 54 \text{ square inches}. \] </think> <answer> 54 </answer>
